headline: Bates & Others v Post Office Ltd — group litigation by over 500 subpostmasters against Post Office Ltd arising from financial losses, wrongful repayment demands and prosecutions attributable to defects in the Defendant's Horizon accounting software.
paragraphs:
This is *Bates & Others v Post Office Ltd*, the group litigation brought in the English High Court in which subpostmasters (SPMs) alleged that the Post Office's Horizon IT system — rolled out from 1999 and developed by Fujitsu — contained bugs, errors and defects that produced spurious accounting shortfalls in branch accounts.
 
Rather than investigating or disclosing those faults, the Post Office required SPMs to repay the apparent shortfalls in full and, between 1999 and 2015, pursued hundreds of prosecutions against them for offences including theft and false accounting.

The 555 Claimants are former SPMs whose contracts with the Post Office stated that they were held personally responsible for all losses through negligence, carelessness or error.
 They advance claims in misrepresentation, breach of contract and breach of good faith, contending that the Defendant concealed known Horizon faults, extracted repayments on the basis of defective data, and wrongly prosecuted them. 
The Post Office denies these allegations, asserting that the Horizon system was and is robust and extremely unlikely to have caused the discrepancies complained of
, and maintains that complaints were investigated adequately through its helpline and audit processes.

Of the 24 pleaded propositions, only 5 are evidentially supported while 17 remain contested; critically, 2 propositions are marked as undermined — the Defendant's denial that it misrepresented Horizon's reliability (P0019) and its denial that the Claimants suffered any recoverable loss (P0023) — indicating that the evidence already obtained tends against the Defendant on both of those points. At a trial readiness figure of 56.2%, the technically complex questions of Horizon's defects, the Defendant's actual state of knowledge, and the adequacy of its investigations remain the central live battlegrounds. None of the EU legal materials retrieved from the Cellar repository is applicable: they address banana import licensing, EU customs duty remission, EU civil service non-contractual liability, Italian company-law accounting penalties and competition-law attribution — none of which engages the domestic contract, misrepresentation and good-faith issues that define this litigation.
